name: Compile-Time Function Evaluation
summary: D's CTFE and C++'s constexpr/consteval converged on the same point from different paths: arbitrary pure functions can run at compile time when their arguments are known. D bet on a dynamic interpreter early; C++ layered formal rules incrementally. End state is similar; path dependencies differ.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: D's CTFE and C++'s constexpr/consteval converged on the same point from different paths: a substantial subset of the language can run at compile time when its arguments are known. The mechanism replaces template metaprogramming (C++) and string-mixin code generation (D) with ordinary code that just happens to run at compile time.

sections.title: D's CTFE
sections.content:
**D's CTFE** (Compile-Time Function Evaluation) executes arbitrary pure D functions at compile time when their arguments are known. Template arguments, `static if` conditions, and `enum` initialisers can all invoke CTFE transparently. The implementation runs a simplified interpreter over D's frontend AST.

The rule is pragmatic: "if it's pure and the arguments are known, it runs at compile time." D bet on a dynamic interpreter early in the language's life and scaled the supported subset over time. Most of D, including allocations and string manipulation, runs in CTFE today.

sections.title: C++ constexpr and consteval
sections.content:
**C++ constexpr / consteval** followed a more conservative trajectory: C++11 introduced `constexpr` for simple expressions, and each standard expanded what's allowed (C++14 added statements, C++17 added more library support, C++20 added `consteval` for mandatory compile-time and limited dynamic allocation during constant evaluation, and C++23 expanded library and language support further). C++26 continues that trajectory.

The interesting difference from D: D bet on a dynamic interpreter early and scaled up; C++ layered formal rules incrementally and caught up. The end state is similar — both let you run much of the language at compile time — but the path dependencies show. Mainstream C++ `constexpr` evaluation is usually implemented by an interpreter/evaluator inside the compiler rather than by JIT- or AOT-compiling the compile-time program.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
Both approaches face the same fundamental trade-off: the more code permitted at compile time, the slower compilation gets and the more compiler bugs surface in unexpected places. C++'s formal rules approach gives sharper error messages when constraints are violated, at the cost of a constantly-evolving rules surface that compilers must implement carefully. D's interpreter approach gives a smoother rollout and broader feature support at the cost of compile-time performance and occasional surprising runtime divergences when CTFE and runtime semantics differ subtly.

For a new language: explicit staging (Zig comptime, MetaOCaml, Mojo `@parameter`) provides clearer mental models than implicit CTFE; CTFE is friendlier to retrofit but harder to reason about formally. The choice tracks whether the language's metaprogramming story is layered onto an existing dynamic-runtime semantics or designed staged from the start.
